But what happens if the property after the dot doesn't exist?
So we learn one thing here: reading a property that doesn't exist gives you
. But what if you go a level deeper? Is that still
obj.status.name; // VM66:1 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
// VM66:1 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
Now you might be wondering why this isn't
too. The error is basically saying "you can't read the
." Think back to what we just saw —
, so the problem is that second-level missing property.

So where exactly does it go wrong? This comes down to operator precedence and associativity. In an earlier chapter we saw that when operators on the same line have the same precedence, the engine falls back on associativity to decide order. The dot operator is left-to-right associative, so the left side gets evaluated first:
, so what actually runs is this:
VM66:1 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
. There's another important point hiding here too: primitive types aren't allowed to have their own properties, so even if you try to add one to
, it won't stick. So that error message really means "you can't add a
undefined.name = 'qq123' // Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set property 'name' of undefined
// Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set property 'name' of undefined
Other primitive types throw slightly different errors though:
// VM309:1 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set property 'name' of null
// Uncaught SyntaxError: Invalid or unexpected token — the dot gets mistaken for a decimal point, so use a variable instead
// no error, but the assignment still doesn't take

There's one more curious thing worth pointing out. If you type
straight into the browser, you'll get
simply doesn't exist. But if you type
instead, you'll be surprised to find it returns

console.log(a); // ReferenceError: a is not defined
console.log(window.a); // undefined
On top of that, you'll notice another thing: once an error is thrown, the code stops immediately and won't keep running afterward.

The difference between these two cases is that accessing a property has a built-in protection mechanism. That mechanism mainly exists to stop you from accessing a property that doesn't exist and triggering a JavaScript error that would halt your code.

Reading a property that doesn't exist on an object hands you undefined — but go one level deeper and suddenly JavaScript throws an error instead. This post digs into why that happens, how operator precedence and associativity cause it, why primitives can't hold their own properties, and the two simple ways to avoid the crash.
